Description: Optimal average power for Plywood surface cleaning without charring
Near-infrared wavelength for optimal Plywood absorption and minimal penetration
Beam spot diameter for precise Plywood surface treatment
Optimal repetition rate for efficient Plywood cleaning coverage
Fluence threshold for effective Plywood contaminant removal
Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled Plywood surface ablation
Optimal scanning speed for uniform Plywood cleaning
Recommended number of passes for complete Plywood cleaning
Optimal beam overlap for uniform Plywood surface treatment
Optimal dwell time per spot for Plywood processing

Low to moderate power prevents charring of plywood's organic layers; starting at 50W for light contamination, max avoids substrate damage (source: studies on 1064 nm cleaning of wood composites, e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2018).

Slower speeds ensure effective removal without overheating; 500 mm/s optimal for uniform cleaning on plywood, adjustable for light (faster) vs heavy (slower) contamination (source: industry manuals from Laserax and IPG Photonics).

Nanosecond pulses preferred over ps/fs for cost-effective ablation with minimal HAZ on low-conductivity plywood; 20 ns balances efficiency and heat control (source: laser physics reviews in Opt. Lasers Eng. 2020).

Moderate rates for controlled energy delivery; 20 kHz recommended to avoid heat accumulation in plywood while enabling faster cleaning than low rates (source: academic literature on pulsed laser cleaning of organics, Appl. Surf. Sci. 2019).

Fluence below plywood's ~1.2 J/cm² damage threshold for safe contaminant ablation; 0.5 J/cm² typical for surface preparation (source: ablation threshold studies for wood, e.g., Wood Sci. Technol. 2017).
Smaller spots for precision on plywood's textured surface; 200 μm recommended for large-area cleaning without excessive overlap issues (source: equipment specs from Coherent and Adapt Laser Systems).

Multi-pass for thorough removal of contaminants without single-pass overheating; 2 passes standard for plywood to minimize damage (source: industry case studies on wood surface preparation, Laser Cleaning Handbook 2021).
Ensures uniform coverage on irregular plywood surfaces; 70% balances efficiency and even cleaning (source: process optimization in J. Clean. Prod. 2022).
